content:
# Mark Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 35v–36r (PDF pages 70–71) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_007_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_007_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ A group of Pharisees, along with some legal experts from Jerusalem, gathered around Jesus.

² They had noticed that some of his disciples ate without first performing the ritual hand-washing.

³ (The Pharisees—and all observant Jews—won't eat without carefully washing their hands, following ancestral tradition.

⁴ When they come from the marketplace, they don't eat until they've purified themselves. They also follow many other traditional practices, like the ceremonial washing of cups, pitchers, and kettles.)

⁵ So the Pharisees and scribes confronted him: "Why don't your disciples follow the tradition of the elders? They eat with ritually unclean hands!"

⁶ He replied, "Isaiah was right when he prophesied about you hypocrites. As it is written: 'These people honor me with their words, but their hearts are far from me.

⁷ Their worship is pointless, because what they teach as divine truth is nothing but human rules.'

⁸ You abandon God's command and cling to human tradition."

⁹ Then he added, "You have quite a talent for sidestepping God's command so you can maintain your own tradition!

¹⁰ Moses said, 'Honor your father and mother,' and, 'Anyone who curses their father or mother must be put to death.'

¹¹ But you allow people to say to their parents, 'Sorry, whatever help I might have given you has been declared Corban'—meaning, 'dedicated to God'—

¹² and from that point on you let them off the hook from doing anything for their parents.

¹³ You cancel God's word with the tradition you've passed down. And this is just one example—you do plenty of things like this."

¹⁴ Then Jesus called the crowd over again and said, "Listen to me, everyone, and take this in:

¹⁵ Nothing that enters a person from outside can make them unclean. It's what comes out of a person that makes them unclean."

¹⁶ [If you have ears, use them!]

¹⁷ After he had left the crowd and gone inside, his disciples asked him to explain.

¹⁸ He said, "Are you really this slow to get it? Don't you see that nothing going into a person from outside can contaminate them?

¹⁹ Food doesn't go into the heart—it goes through the stomach and out of the body." (By saying this, Jesus declared that all foods are acceptable to eat.)

²⁰ Then he continued, "What comes out of a person—that's what contaminates them.

²¹ From deep inside—from the human heart—come evil thoughts: sexual immorality, theft, murder,

²² adultery, greed, malice, deception, indulgence, jealousy, slander, arrogance, and recklessness.

²³ All this poison comes from within, and it is what makes a person unclean."

²⁴ Jesus left that area and traveled to the region of Tyre. He found a house to stay in and wanted to keep his presence quiet, but it was impossible.

²⁵ Almost immediately, a woman whose young daughter was tormented by an evil spirit heard he was there. She came and fell at his feet.

²⁶ She was a Greek—a non-Jew from Syrophoenicia. She begged him to drive the demon out of her daughter.

²⁷ He told her, "First let the children eat and be satisfied. It isn't right to take the children's food and toss it to the dogs."

²⁸ She shot back, "That's true, Lord. But even the dogs under the table get to eat the children's scraps."

²⁹ He said, "Because you said that, go home. The demon has left your daughter."

³⁰ She went home and found her little girl lying peacefully on the bed. The demon was gone.

³¹ From Tyre, Jesus traveled through Sidon and down to the Sea of Galilee, passing through the Decapolis region.

³² People brought him a man who was deaf and could barely speak. They begged Jesus to place his hands on him.

³³ Jesus took the man aside, away from the crowd. He put his fingers into the man's ears, then spit and touched the man's tongue.

³⁴ He looked up to heaven with a deep sigh and said, "*Ephphatha!*"—an Aramaic word meaning "Open up!"

³⁵ Instantly the man's ears opened, his tongue was freed, and he began speaking clearly.

³⁶ Jesus ordered them not to tell anyone. But the more he told them to be quiet, the more they spread the news.

³⁷ People were overwhelmed with amazement. "Everything he does is wonderful!" they said. "He makes the deaf hear and the speechless talk!"

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"You have quite a talent for sidestepping God's command" (v. 9):** The Greek is dripping with sarcasm — ΚΑΛΩΣ ("nicely," "beautifully") is used ironically. Jesus is essentially saying, "What an excellent job you do at nullifying God's law." I tried to capture the bite.

2. **The Corban loophole (vv. 10-13):** This is a devastating example. A person could dedicate money to the temple (declaring it "Corban") to avoid spending it on aging parents—while potentially still benefiting from it themselves. It's religion weaponized against human decency.

3. **"All foods are acceptable to eat" (v. 19):** This editorial parenthesis is Mark's theological bombshell. It retrojects the early church's conclusion about food laws back to Jesus's teaching. Whether Jesus himself intended this sweeping conclusion or Mark is drawing out the logical implication is one of the great debates in NT scholarship.

4. **"She shot back" (v. 28):** The woman doesn't grovel. She takes Jesus's metaphor and turns it on him with sharp wit. This is one of the only times in the Gospels where someone "wins" a verbal exchange with Jesus—and he praises her for it.

5. **The physical details of the healing (vv. 33-34):** Jesus uses touch, saliva, and an Aramaic command. These physical, almost folk-healing details are unique to Mark and were later omitted by Matthew and Luke—possibly because they found them too crude for Jesus. Mark preserves them because they happened.

6. **"He makes the deaf hear and the speechless talk" (v. 37):** This echoes Isaiah 35:5-6, a messianic prophecy about the age of restoration. The crowd is inadvertently confessing that the messianic age has arrived.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
